text: The surgeon has entered the peritoneal cavity, revealing a large central liver tumor prominently visible in the center of the screen. To the left of the screen, near the gallbladder, an extension of the tumor remains viable following embolization.

The surgeon uses the Thunderbeat device to mobilize the falciform ligament and separate the anterior surface of the liver from the anterior abdominal wall. The Thunderbeat effectively transects the liver tissue, providing hemostasis. An intraoperative ultrasound is performed to assess the extent of the liver lesion and guide the resection for negative margins.

The surgeon uses the Thunderbeat device to meticulously resect the liver parenchyma, achieving clean cuts with minimal bleeding. The gallbladder is partially moved to facilitate complete tumor removal. Blood is visible on the left side of the screen, but the Olympus IV case system maintains clear visualization of the surgical field, allowing observation of tissue planes unfolding during resection.

The surgeon achieves excellent hemostasis using the Thunderbeat device, despite minor nuisance bleeding. The majority of the liver resection is performed with this tool. A branch of the middle hepatic vein is gently dissected, with visible thrombosed pores in the vein.

Blood is pooling in the surgical field, originating from a small hole in a branch of the middle hepatic vein. The surgeon uses the Thunderbeat device in bipolar mode to seal the vein, initially unsuccessful but achieving hemostasis on the second attempt. Despite the red field, anatomical details remain visible.

The surgical field displays excellent color detail and contrast, highlighting yellows, browns, and reds. A small piece of cellulose film is placed to absorb excess fluid. The inflow pedicle to segment four is isolated, and a tunnel is bluntly created to safely introduce a stapler, cutting off the blood supply to segment four.

The surgeon fires the stapler, revealing the resection plane with clear visualization despite minor oozing and nuisance bleeding. The liver tissue displays distinct color variations, and visible branches of blood vessels extend into the tumor. The surgeon probes these vessels to determine the necessary steps for safe resection.

The surgical field appears relatively bloodless as the surgeon identifies branches entering the specimen that require ligation. Clips are placed on the staying side to prevent bile leaks, while the Thunderbeat is used to dissect through the specimen side, ensuring hemostasis. The Thunderbeat efficiently cuts through the liver parenchyma, except near the staple line, where a stapler is used for safety. The stapler is positioned, closed, and fired, completing the vascular ligation. The Thunderbeat is then used to unfold the remaining tissue layers of the resection.

The surgical field is clearly visible despite significant blood staining, allowing the surgeon to identify and clip individual branches before safely dividing them. The specimen is manipulated to reveal its final attachments, with most of the resection completed using the Thunderbeat, supplemented by a few clips and two stapler firings.

The surgeon completes the resection, revealing streams of blood from the liver surface. The gallbladder specimen is moved aside following cholecystectomy to assist with hemostasis. Topical hemostatic agents are applied, and a sponge is placed on top to apply pressure for additional hemostasis.

The surgical field is observed after the extraction of the specimen through a fan and steel incision, with no pneumoperitoneum present. The field is inspected for any signs of bleeding, and the appearance is dry and satisfactory.
